From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

England international rugby union player & British Army officer

Rugby player

Dickie Guest

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Full name | Richard Heaton Guest |
| Born | (1918-03-12)12 March 1918  [Prescot](/wiki/Prescot "Prescot"), England |
| Died | May 2012 (aged 94) |
| Rugby union career | |
| |  |  |  |  | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **Position** | Wing | | | | International career | | | | | **Years** | **Team** | **Apps** | **(Points)** | | 1939–49 | [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") | 13 | (15) | | |

**Richard Heaton Guest** (12 March 1918 – May 2012) was an English international [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") player.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Born in [Prescot](/wiki/Prescot "Prescot"), Guest was educated at Cowley School, St Helens, and [Liverpool University](/wiki/Liverpool_University "Liverpool University").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Guest, a [Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo_FC "Waterloo FC") winger, was still a teenager when he made his representative debut for [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire_RFU "Lancashire RFU") and won the first of his four County titles in 1938, on his 20th birthday.[[3]](#cite_note-WorldRugbyMuseum-3)

In 1939, Guest broke into the [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") line up, featuring on the wing in all three of their Home Nations matches.[[3]](#cite_note-WorldRugbyMuseum-3)

Guest served as a [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery") captain during World War II and in 1942 was attached to the Sudan Defence Forces.[[3]](#cite_note-WorldRugbyMuseum-3)

From 1947 to 1949, Guest was capped a further nine times for England, making him one of only three to have represented the team both sides of the war, along with [Jack Heaton](/wiki/Jack_Heaton_(rugby_union) "Jack Heaton (rugby union)") (his cousin) and [Tommy Kemp](/wiki/Tommy_Kemp "Tommy Kemp")).[[3]](#cite_note-WorldRugbyMuseum-3)

Guest was an England selector between 1963 and 1966.[[3]](#cite_note-WorldRugbyMuseum-3)
